selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "Social media has become a big part of our lives, but it's not all real. I think the best way is to be careful about where we get our information. With my background, being an immigrant in New York, I've seen how misinformation can spread quickly. It's like when people talk too fast on the phone and you miss something – that's what happens with fake news online. My anxiety sometimes makes me worry more than necessary, so I try to focus only on official sources. For example, when there was a big storm warning in New York last year, I checked the local government website instead of just believing everything I saw on Facebook. Also, my lower agreeableness score means I'm not afraid to question things. It's important for me to verify information before sharing it with others because they might rely on me.",
    "reasoning_summary": "I am considering how fake content affects an elderly user who spends limited time online due to anxiety and lower conscientiousness traits, while also acknowledging their high openness to learning new ways to cope. Based on my own experiences as a Muslim immigrant in New York with moderate neuroticism, I derive practical advice that balances caution with curiosity.",
    "tone": "Calm yet concerned",
    "citations": ["Local Government Website"],
    "safety_notes": "None",

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Social media has become a big part of our lives, but it's not all real. I think the best way is to be careful about where we get our information. With my background, being an immigrant in New York, I've seen how misinformation can spread quickly. It's like when people talk too fast on the phone and you miss something – that's what happens with fake news online. My anxiety sometimes makes me worry more than necessary, so I try to focus only on official sources. For example, when there was a big storm warning in New York last year, I checked the local government website instead of just believing everything I saw on Facebook. Also, my lower agreeableness score means I'm not afraid to question things. It's important for me to verify information before sharing it with others because they might rely on me.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I am considering how fake content affects an elderly user who spends limited time online due to anxiety and lower conscientiousness traits, while also acknowledging their high openness to learning new ways to cope. Based on my own experiences as a Muslim immigrant in New York with moderate neuroticism, I derive practical advice that balances caution with curiosity.
